tests.title: Correct build under test
tests.requires: You installed the release intended for this test pass.
tests.expected: The control panel version banner shows the build under test (0.10.2), so every result maps to the right code. A stale install must never masquerade as a bug.

steps.text: Open the control panel (http://127.0.0.1:8765) or the packaged app window.
Confirm the version banner reads 0.10.2 - it must match the installer you just ran.
If it does NOT match, STOP and install the correct build first - results from a stale build are invalid.

tests.title: Push-to-talk round trip: cues, state machine, spoken reply
tests.requires: App running with mic + speakers; panel open; personality ON.
tests.expected: A held-key utterance flows LISTENING→TRANSCRIBING→THINKING→SPEAKING→IDLE with the right cue at each stage and a spoken reply that addresses you as "Commander".

steps.text: Hold `[` → you hear a `listen` cue immediately (before speaking).
While holding, say "Hello COVAS, can you hear me? Keep it short." then release → you hear a `processing` cue.
Panel status + log move through LISTENING → TRANSCRIBING → THINKING → SPEAKING → IDLE.
Your words appear as `Commander: …` and the reply as `COVAS: …` (timestamped) in the log.
Just before speech you hear the `completed` cue, then the reply plays in the ElevenLabs voice and addresses you as "Commander".

tests.title: Tap-to-cancel, barge-in, and panel CANCEL all interrupt
tests.requires: App running with mic + speakers; panel open.
tests.expected: A brief tap of `[` (<400 ms) stops thinking/speaking instantly; holding `[` during a reply barges in with a fresh capture; the panel CANCEL button also stops a reply.

steps.text: Ask a long question ("Tell me the history of the Elite Dangerous galaxy in detail."). While it is thinking or speaking, tap `[` briefly → it stops instantly and returns to IDLE.
Confirm a normal hold still records fine (a hold is well over the 400 ms tap threshold).
Barge-in: while a reply is being spoken, hold `[` again → speech cuts off and a fresh capture starts.
The panel's CANCEL / STOP button also stops an in-progress reply.

tests.title: Router picks the cheap tier by default and escalates on demand
tests.requires: `[router].enabled = true`; app running; panel open (read the `[router]` and `[usage]` log lines).
tests.expected: Each turn logs a `[router] [<tier>] <model> max_tokens=N — <reason>` line (the tier prefix is from issue #11) and a `[usage] … [<model>]` line; banter uses [cheap] Haiku, depth/"think hard" escalates to [standard] Sonnet, explicit "use Opus" uses [premium] Opus, and "full breakdown" raises max_tokens.

steps.text: Banter cheap tier: "Morning, COVAS — how's it going?" → router line shows `[cheap] claude-haiku-4-5`; cost a fraction of a cent.
"Think hard about the best way to break in a new ship." → `[standard] claude-sonnet-5`.
Depth phrase: "Walk me through the pros and cons of a fuel scoop." → [standard] Sonnet.
Explicit premium: "Use Opus for this — summarize the Thargoid war." → `[premium] claude-opus-4-8`.
"Give me the full breakdown of the engineering process." → higher `max_tokens` (2048).
(Optional) Set the router pin in Settings (`cheap`/`standard`/`premium`, or `haiku`/`sonnet`/`opus`) and confirm the router line's tier + model reflect it.

tests.title: Automatic web search on current-info questions, capped and cancelable
tests.requires: `[web_search].enabled = true`; app running; panel open; internet.
tests.expected: A current-info question triggers a logged web search (with a processing beep), the answer reflects live info, mid-search tap-cancel stops it, and searches are capped at `max_uses` (3) per reply.

steps.text: "What's the latest Elite Dangerous update right now?" → log shows "Searching the web for …", status hits a searching state, you hear a processing beep.
The spoken answer reflects live/current info.
Cancel mid-search: start another current-info question, then tap `[` while searching → it stops.
Searches are capped at `[web_search].max_uses` (3) per reply.

tests.title: Odd input, session log, provider hiccup, clean quit
tests.expected: A long/complex reply streams + speaks and the loop SURVIVES without the console crashing (genuine non-cp1252 glyph safety is covered by an offline unit test); the newest `logs\session_*.log` holds timestamped prompts/replies + router/usage; a brief network kill degrades gracefully back to IDLE (dead TTS falls back to text); Ctrl+Alt+Q shuts down cleanly.

steps.text: Odd/long reply does not crash the loop: ask for a punctuation- and symbol-heavy answer (e.g. "Explain em dashes, en dashes, hyphens, and arrows, with examples."). COVAS streams + speaks the whole reply and returns to IDLE with no console crash. NOTE: it is a voice app — the model describes symbols in words and STT cannot feed it glyphs, so you are confirming the loop SURVIVES any reply, not that specific glyphs print. Real non-cp1252 console safety is covered by the offline unit test (tests/test_console_hardening.py).
Open the newest `logs\session_*.log` → prompts + replies with timestamps, plus router/usage lines.
A provider hiccup (briefly kill network) degrades gracefully — the loop survives and returns to IDLE; a dead TTS falls back to text.
Ctrl+Alt+Q (or closing the console window) shuts it down cleanly.

runs.session: 01 voice-loop-core: PTT->STT->LLM->TTS, barge-in, cost router, web search, robustness

failure.notes: Draw me an ASCII arrow and explain it - failed
failure.console:

14:04:26 router: [cheap] claude-haiku-4-5 max_tokens=1024 — default -> cheap tier
14:04:26 » Thinking
14:04:28 usage: in=1648 out=108 cache_write=0 cache_read=13270 ~$0.0035 [claude-haiku-4-5]
14:04:28 COVAS: I don't have real-time update information, Commander — that's out of my reach. You'd need to check the launcher or the official forums for what's current as of today.

As for the ASCII arrow: can't do it. I'm speaking to you via audio, so there's no visual — no ASCII art, no diagrams, no text formatting. Just words. If you need to see something drawn out, you'll want a screen for that.

Anything else I can help with?
14:04:29 » Speaking

runs.session: 01 voice-loop-core re-test on v0.10.1 (history-integrity fix): build-check, web-search FIRST real run, robustness s1-s4

failure.notes: "Ask for a reply that MUST contain odd Unicode ("Say these words back to me — café, naïve, jalapeño". No unicode characters were printed. I assume they were supposed to be? It wasn't totally clear.
failure.console:
16:37:42 Commander: Say these words back to me. Cafe, naive, jalapeno. Then tell me what the right words arrow and m-dash symbols are called.
16:37:42 router: [cheap] claude-haiku-4-5 max_tokens=1024 — default -> cheap tier
16:37:42 » Thinking
16:37:45 usage: in=2608 out=131 cache_write=0 cache_read=13270 ~$0.0046 [claude-haiku-4-5]
16:37:45 COVAS: Cafe, naive, jalapeno. Easy enough.

The right-pointing arrow symbol is called a right arrow, or if you want the formal name, a rightwards arrow. The m-dash — that's the long horizontal line used in prose — is called an em dash. It's called that because it's roughly the width of the letter M in the typeface you're using. There's also an en dash, which is shorter, about the width of an N, and a hyphen, which is shorter still — but the m-dash is the one for setting off clauses or adding emphasis in writing.

runs.session: 01 robustness re-walk on v0.10.1: s1 reframed (unicode now a unit test), confirm s2 session-log, s3 network-kill degrade, s4 clean quit
